text: > Why not do some tests? Do blood work, MRIs, CT scans, see if and how she functions differently vs non-immuned people. The game doesn't tell us very much, but even then, you can see xrays and other types of imaging in the OR when Joel walks in. So yes, they probably did some tests. But sometimes, you can't figure out what's really happening unless you get a sample of it. That just how science works unfortunately. And the brain just so happens to be one of the trickiest organs to sample. In a normal world, they would take all the time needed to figure something out. But this wasn't a normal world. This was a world that was decimated by a killer fungus and had left humans fighting each other for scraps just to survive. In a world where one scratch, or even a sniff of the spores, is a death sentence, your options run out very quickly. You do what you need to do.
label: analysis

text: > It was literally only a few hours after Joel and Ellie were knocked out... It doesn't take that long to do imaging on an unconcious patient. And we don't actually know how much time passed between them getting found and Joel waking up. Joel wasn't knocked out, only Ellie was. For all we know, he probably had some time to eat something and sleep for a few hours while Ellie was being treated. > No competent scientist would have immediately thought of killing their test subject and ruining the only opportunity you might have. Again, it's about options. They barely had enough resources to keep the OR running, not to even mention the entire hospital. Do you know how much domain expertise it takes to run a tetiary institution? You make do with what you have. Not to mention that the Fireflies were in an all out war with FEDRA. In an ideal world, you wouldn't need to smuggle a person like Ellie out of Boston, or even commit her survival into the hands of a burned out carpenter-turned contraband smuggler. No, she would be escorted in a freaking military motorcade, and have her every whim attended to like she was the princess of the planet. So yeah, you're not wrong. You're just failing to account for the reality of the world they exist in. Plus, this was something they'd been looking into for a while. They had a research lab at ECU that was destroyed, remember. They were waiting on Ellie for quite a while by the time she arrived. So it's fair to expect that they already had all their ducks in a row.
label: analysis

text: The game earns the player's investment in the characters, which makes the personal interactions much more interesting and important. The show version of the story is so compressed and brief, then when the show takes its time to dwell on the personal interactions, these really don't feel like they have been earned yet because the viewer hasn't been given enough reason to be that invested in the characters. The show itself seems to rush through a checklist of the game's plot points and details, which are not in full context, and it does not surprise me when I see people discussing parts of the plot the way you have. Season one had this problem to a degree but it was enough of a straight-forward story focused on very few characters that I think it was an easier sell even in its compressed format. The game Part II though is more like a big complicated and rich novel with several more important characters and relationships than the first game that season one adapts. This show just doesn't have the time to make these things work.
label: analysis

text: The show actually had more mentions of space than the game did I think. She mentions she wants to go to the moon in season 1 and Joel and her bond over him knowing who her favorite astronaut is, she later says they can live in a sheep farm on the moon, she has space stuff in her room and she mentions astronauts who died before takeoff when they find the tank a couple episodes ago. Plus the scifi comic book back in season 1. And I’ve never really been into “react” content on youtube but I make the exception for TLoU just cause I want to know what non fans think of it since they’re going in fresh and across the board, everyone seems to really buy into Joel and Ellie’s dynamic almost immediately but especially by the time they make it to Jackson in season 1. It feels different cause in the game, there’s definitely more survival but most of the 30+ hours was video game-y stuff. And that’s the kinda stuff regular people just filled in the gaps with. There’s an idea that you need a prolonged time with characters to actually like them but that’s actually not true, or movies would never have any connections with audiences. The reality is you have about 2-3 scenes or about 15 minutes tops to get onboard with a character, but it’s usually done in their first few lines. That’s why most characters’ first scenes are them being funny or brave or cute or heroic or even put in a bad spot. More connection and understanding can be grown from there but if you need more than several movies worth of screentime to care about a character then there’s just a fundamental misunderstanding of how easily humans connect to things. Put two googly eyes on a rock and you’ll have people immediately like that rock. Like as a random example, the character of Beetlejuice has only existed in live action less than 30 minutes and he’s one of the most famous characters ever created. Stand By Me is better than most things ever made and it’s almost as long as the pilot episode of TLoU. For any proof of its execution, this episode was the highest rated episode of the season by critics and most critics didn’t even play the game. But yeah, I’m a writer who loves the games so it’s just fascinating to me how regular people are absorbing the show and what’s the response to the changes along the way. Personally, I think I’ll always enjoy the game more but I’ve been really enjoying the show and I don’t think I’ll ever stop dissecting it and the response to it.
label: analysis

text: Live audiences laughing when a scene is genuinely funny is one thing. Late night talk shows have been making that work forever. BBT is just, not in my opinion, a funny show, and the laughs somehow make it worse. We get it, they're nerds. OMG he said Klingon! LoL! Same thing goes for Georgie and Mandy. Young Sheldon was funny on its own. When they went to the new series, the writing got worse, and the laugh track feels like a cheap way to make up for it. Mash, Cheers, Seinfeld, Frasier, Roseanne, King of Queens, etc, are all great shows, and the laugh track adds something. Take Tim Allen as a case study. Home Improvement and Last Man Standing were funny shows, and the laugh track felt genuine. Shifting Gears is not funny, and the laugh track feels inauthentic and jarring.
label: hot_take

text: Oh, don't forget that if it's from ENT or earlier it'll also have something racially or sexually problematic on top of the actual sack handling, along with some random sexism. Something like: **Act 1:** One of the female crewmembers is shown to have problems communicating with other women. They arrive on the planet, and find out about the sack tapping (which is lauded by the men for how straight-forward it is). **Act 2:** The crewmember falls in with the all-female anti-sack tapping resistance, who only wants to introduce a new form of communication that would allow women to also speak to each other rather than men having a monopoly on all communications. **Act 3:** The crewmember tries to help the resistance with some protest despite the captain telling her not to, and they fail. The captain gives her some small token punishment and is like "I know how you feel, but it's their culture and not our place to intervene unless their [all-male] government requests it." It ends with the captain watching as the crewmember communicates more confidently with other women on the ship, and we see them give a content smile as they tell the log that the trip did bring some good after all.
label: reaction

text: Letting those kids into the airport to practice was so incredibly sweet. That wasn't Nathan being "Nathan For You" Nathan, that was the real Nathan coming out. You can tell because he didn't spend an exorbitant amount of time on it in the show, however, that was probably a whole days worth of doing that with those kids. I think Nathan is funny beyond belief, literally a comedic genius, but he's also incredibly thoughtful and wicked smart. The way he blurs the line between comedy and actual research/ doing something actually good is so crazy. My husband and I just love the guy. Side note: both congressmen kind of sucked, but I'm not all that shocked really. I can't remember the last time a politician actually cared about doing anything good that wasn't wrapped up in their own self interests. The way the original one he was trying to contact said no to a meeting- um, no sir, you work for us. Taking meetings with the people you work for is part of the job. And the Cohen dude... yuck. The way he barely paid attention to him at all, and then just cut him off at the end was basically like, "Good day to you, sir" was so unprofessional. Nathan may do weird, silly, and insane things, but he is trying to do something good and this idea that just because he's a professional "funny man" means that he can't be smart or do anything serious or good is kind of ridiculous. Plus, the fact that the guy claims to be all about autism yet doesn't even know a basic term like masking is laughable. I'm not autistic and even I knew about it.
label: hot_take

text: Narratively, I don't think it really matters. Like Joel says at the end of the first game, "You keep finding something to fight for...". It doesn't matter if the vaccine has little to no chance. For the Fireflies that's why they fight, live, and look for the light. They need to pursue a cure because that is their reason for surviving. Even on the smallest of scales, it's meaningful for why they push on. There wasn't a scenario once Ellie shows up where Ellie doesn't die to desperate people at St. Mary's. They have to take that chance in their ever darkening and shrinking world view. By the time we get to hospital they've been losing people, flailing from place to place, and not really winning any ground towards their goals. Contextually, it's not just meaningful anymore, it's all there is. Joel definitively ends the means to a far-fetched cure ,as unbeknownst to them some smuggler needs her just as much as they do, but he shatters them by smothering their final cinder. So I'm not sure the cure is ever truly meaningful beyond the fireflies need to believe in it, Ellie's search for her own worth despite so much loss, and as a simple catalyst to set things in motion in Boston. Which is why it's so weird that the show made it so concrete and less complex of a scenario. But who knows if they will punch holes in that next season with other flashbacks.
label: analysis

text: I’m not saying that just because the creator likes it it’s perfect. I’m saying it’s unfair that so many people are throwing hate at Craig and saying Neil gets the story and he doesn’t, when they’re working closely together. Okay, but have you asked yourself why they might’ve done it? Cause once you look at it through the tv lens, this is a necessary change. Games and TV are formatted differently. Out of 90% of the changes, this one makes the most sense. Not to mention, that’s not the climax. In the game, that’s the denouement. The climax is Ellie letting Abby live, that’s when the thesis of the game truly comes to fruition and when Ellie makes a choice in accordance with that thesis. Not to mention, you didn’t know the porch scene would be in this episode, so you don’t know how the ending will be. I don’t think it’s crazy to say some of the changes are absolutely better, even in this season (and I agree the season so far isn’t as good as the game. But imo it’s still amazing TV). But there’s always a change the ending on the show will be better. The writers see the full picture, we don’t.
label: analysis

text: I don’t really post on Reddit or social media in general but I have to tell this to someone. Ben stiller is literally one of the best directors I’ve seen, and I am not only talking about the script and how brings out the best in the actors or how the story falls perfectly in place. I am currently watching severance and boy does he know how to frame a scene, I mean observe closely every single frame in the series literally every single one can be an award winning photograph! Literally all the scenes even the scenes where the characters are sprinting through the hallways, the camera is at a perfect angle the character/s are perfectly in the middle square and don’t even get me started on the symmetry in almost every frame. I mean it’s not all him, the DP Jessica Lee Gagne is the main brain behind this but he worked very closely and set the visual tone for the show. If it’s not too bold, if you look at it from a pure perspective of cinematography and filmography in framing of a scene,the show is very close to the visual experience of a Wes Anderson film, the tone of the script and narrative is different but if I literally just screenshot a scene from severance and a Wes Anderson film and filter it to black and white it’s very similar framing.
label: analysis
note: craft observations + names DP; gushing but grounded

text: So they knew how they'd mass produce and distribute the vaccine? Especially without the military's assistance. In our current world, there was a struggle to get the Covid vaccines mass produced quickly. You think 20 people would have the capacity to make vaccines for millions of people around the would? (Even with a 99% mortality rate, that'd still be 80 million people...). > Not to mention that the Fireflies were in an all out war with FEDRA The military would likely have the resources to mass produce and distribute the vaccine. The CDC still exists in the game's world. Coordinate a ceasefire and cooperate to test the cure (no competent scientist/researcher would let a cure or vaccine in the wild without even one trial or test), and mass produce and distribute it. But no, these 20 people were humanity's *only hope* as implied in the second game. It's ridiculous even to type that. > For all we know, he probably had some time to eat something and sleep for a few hours while Ellie was being treated. So...a day or two? What competent scientist would kill literally the one person that could lead to a cure after one or two days of doing tests? If you kill her and nothing came from it, you're screwed. Oopsie. It should only be absolute the last resort. Like they're super desperate after doing every test imaginable. Like i wrote in one or two of the other comments, this would have easily been fixable by setting the climas a year after Joel and Ellie arrive at the Firefly HQ. Hell, most of the dialogue wouldn't need to be changed either.

text: Of course. To understand the ending of Mad Men, particularly the Don Draper ending, we must observe how ads are used in Msd Men, and for what purpose, and we must observe the ending of the other characters. All the other characters worked through their inner struggles of the last 10 years and came out nenewed and grown. Roger confronted his aging and fear of death, and settled with a woman his own age; Joan shed her beleif she was dependand on men and set out on her own; Peggy who struggle to be recognized as a person finally fell in with Stan, who saw her as a person, as opposed to all her other boyfriends. Pete shed all his trappings of a New York blue blood (even New York itself!) and became his own self made man. Even Betty, being forced to confront death, is maturing. Since all the other characters found resolution, it's logical to assume Don Draper did as well - and then scene with Leoanard is it's ultimate catalycist. Throughout the show's run, the ads were being used to illustrate a point or a theme of the episode. Mad Men wasn't really a show \*about\* advertising, it was \*using\* advertising to underline it's themes. Don's advertising was progressively getting darker and darker, reflecting upon his inner struggles. By series end, Don Draper went west and lost every identifying marker of Don Draper: Sterling Cooper agency didn't exist anymore, he gave away his money, his car. Then he embraced Leonard, and embraced and accepted himself. We hear a ting. Don's final ad then is about harmony and joy, indicating he found inner peace. The complete opposite of cynicism.
label: analysis

text: I'll agree that both versions of the text have their own strengths - I think there are things the show is doing that is better than the game - BUT it also highlights my point, the differences in the show all serve the way that the story is being told because it's one 45 minute TV show in a season of 10 and not a self-paced chapter of a 30 hour video game. It's Dina _not_ Tommy so to set up the (honestly) better evolution of the Dina/Ellie relationship, to shortcut how they learn about the WLF, and to put Tommy back in the town so we can a wider understanding of Jackson and not just through Ellie, Jesse and Dina's viewpoints. We couldn't have ever seen that in the game because we're only ever with Joel, Ellie or Abby. Abby explicitly sets out her motivations to camera because we didn't have the time _with_ her before this to learn them through the gameplay. The cabin? I can't justify that change, maybe its production related, maybe having the Seattle 5 and Ellie and Joel and Dina was too hard to light properly, maybe it makes sense because that's where they slept.
label: analysis

text: One of the better episodes this season. I enjoyed how they used Eugene's death as a way for Ellie to confirm that Joel was lying about the Fireflies/Salt Lake City. I also enjoyed the expansion on Joel's childhood, etc The line where Eugene is begging to see his wife's face is ironic, in a few ways. I imagined Eugene envisioned a relatively dignified end at his wife's side, had he returned to Jackson. Conversely, Joel got anything but that, and died a beaten mess on the floor. I do wonder - was Joel grateful to see his surrogate daughter one last time before dying, or did he feel shame at her seeing him as a tortured husk, especially considering this is all in the aftermath of the conversation they had the previous night? This season has been mixed so far - we'll see how Episode 7 is next week.

text: Yep, I wrote about how stupid the Fireflies' plan was in another comment. They had Ellie for less than a day, and the first thing they do is immediately kill her. Not do any tests on her, studies and experiments, etc that last more than literally *one day*. No competent scientist/researcher would think like this, it's incomprehensible. Wouldn't you want to see how how brain and body react to getting infected? Compare blood tests, CT scans and MRIs, etc, extract some cranial fluid from the spinal tap (which wouldn't kill her), and study it, etc? But no, we have to suspend our disbelief, especially in the second game, that these 20 people could somehow create, mass produce, and distribute the cure *around the world*. Even with a 99% mortality rate, they'd still have to produce 80 million cures. Look at vaccine skepticism now, how will they convince everyone to take it by saying they got it from a single subject without trials or studies? Would you trust a random militia of 20 people that they created a vaccine from one girl? And that's not even factoring in what happens if the fungi mutates to be immune from whatever they create. How does her body react to the fungi's mutation? What if there are side effects to the cure? It falls apart the more you think about it. I've thought about how stupid it is since playing it on the PS3. But the second game/season has everyone act like this was the *only way* to save humanity. Like there are no other people around the world that are trying to find a cure? Ellie is literally a once in a lifetime opportunity. People are acting like it's a binary thing. But Joel's actions in killing everyone is wrong *and* the Fireflies' plan is incredibly stupid and illogical. And the funny thing is that they could have fixed it by setting the climax a year after they arrive at the hospital/HQ. Show and/or imply that they've done many tests and experiments and they've resorted to this. Hell, you don't even have to change the majority of the ending and dialogue except for maybe some more things in the background in parts of the hospital and labs.
label: analysis

text: Honestly, I lost most interest in this show. This episode, though, was a reminder of how essential Joel’s dynamic and existence was to advancing the story that *I cared about hearing* instead of the story that the *show wants to tell me*. I’ll be blunt; I don’t know how you rectify that issue. This episode drove home that I actually just don’t care, at all, about Ellie or Dina or Abby or anyone else, for that matter. The part of the show that struck a chord with me was Joel’s arc, and with him gone, I realized that this show probably won’t ever have anything else for *me* as a viewer that I care about or connect strongly with. The rest of the season has been a meandering mess of inconsistency, tone breaks, and pacing failures…. But it’s because they’re trying to figure out ways to make me care about who is left… and I just don’t. The best example I came give of this sensation is when you have a group of friends that you fall out of touch with. It’s not necessarily *”Fuck you”*, it’s not necessarily hostile or malicious, it’s just more of a… well, that’s that I guess. I say this as an old male viewer. This show just doesn’t have anything left for me as a viewer. I can’t, and don’t, really care that much about a lot of the tertiary stuff. I don’t care about LGBTQI issues, I don’t care much about the romance arc(s), I don’t care much for Ellie as she has been portrayed. And you know what, that’s fine; there is plenty of other entertainment out there for me, but I think this episode really helped me recognize something that I would bet a lot of other male viewers are going through too, which is that the game cared about my demographic a lot, and the television series… well, it doesn’t. To lose Joel all over again, it’s just a reminder of what a connection I had with the game and this series that can’t/doesn’t/won’t exist again. That bums me out the most.
label: hot_take

text: There are two things happening here. Ellie knew it wasn't a done deal that they could get a cure. I mean, who could really say for sure? But it was Ellie's choice. She made her choice and Joel took it away from her because he was selfish. He says he loves her and that's true, but he was sparing himself of the pain of losing Ellie. It wasn't a selfless decision, and he robbed Ellie from her decision and robbed the world of a possible cure. That is the main issue, not whether or not it was possible, but that Ellie chose and Joel decided otherwise. It is in clear contrast with what he does with Eugene. With Eugene, even when he knows they could make it work for him to see Gail, Joel knows protocol. It is too dangerous to take him to see Gail so he does what has to be down and shoots Eugene. A typical case of "do as I say, not as I do". Joel enters in a contradiction. So yeah, whether or not there cure was feasible was beside the point

text: It makes no sense that the doctors and researchers would immediately jump to doing a procedure that'll kill her. Why not do some tests? Do blood work, MRIs, CT scans, see if and how she functions differently vs non-immuned people. Make killing her via an operation the *very* last resort. Instead, it's literally the first thing they think of. And even if they somehow they're successful, *then what??*. How will they mass produce it? How will they distribute it? How will they convince people to take it? How will they be certain it won't mutate to be immune to the vaccine? How will they transport if overseas? Will they cooperate with the US military? They have the resources, especially since it's told in the background that CDC is still active. It's even stupider that they're only 20 people...these twenty people are killed and suddenly the movement is dead...
label: analysis

text: Couldn't agree more. Neil Duckmann has nailed this episode. It is good, progressive storytelling that shows how the love-hate relationship between Ellie and Joel developed over the years. It shows how Joel is very restrictive about his new parenthood, if we can call it that, and how he embraces it with all its nuances as the years go by. Like any parent would, he is worried about teenage activities and fearing to lose his child in the disguise of personal space. And in between everything, the episode also shows how Ellie grows as a person, questioning his suspicions to Joel in a subtle way. For me, the porch scene is immensely satisfying and heartbreaking as well. Yes, I hear people talking about how it should have been in the end, but I think the creators established enough precedence in their relationship to let us believe how everything was not right between the two. Pedro Pascal delivers a stellar, mind-blowing depiction of the scene. Having watched episode 2 beforehand, now you can relate to how he (Joel) had felt in the last days of his demise (despite him not knowing he was gonna die), but at the same time, he (Joel) must have felt a little sense of closure about coming clean to Ellie. And most importantly - saying to her that he loves her. P.S - I absolutely agree that this episode's Ellie is the closest we have seen Ellie from season one. Top stuff. Can't wait for the finale and how it sets up for the season 3 to come. Cheers.
label: analysis

text: What’s your point—That the ending of the first game was dogshit because they didn’t take the time to make the development of the cure believable? Or maybe its that it was never intended to be a real choice Joel had to make because it was so unbelievably obvious that the fireflies could never do it, which I guess would still be a dogshit ending because that removes any semblance of critical engagement with the scenario presented and doesn’t actually tell us anything we don’t know about Joel & Ellie’s Dynamic. I tend to believe that we aren’t supposed to think about a lot of the granular logistical elements that you’re mentioning when it comes to the efficacy of the vaccine, and that its just supposed to be a “Would you sacrifice your child to save the world” scenario, but even if it wasn’t then retconning was the right move. Because the only way that ending even means anything is if the quandary is real. If it’s not then it’s a complete waste of everyone’s time. If it’s the former then they should have spent a lot more time making the vaccine development believable, so still a failure of the writing team.
